items.q: What is the difference between hardness and toughness?
items.correct: Hardness is resistance to scratching and indentation; toughness is the ability to absorb impact without fracturing. Hard materials are often brittle rather than tough.
items.misconception: A hard material is automatically a tough material.
items.random_wrong: Toughness measures how well a material resists rusting.
items.why: Everyday language treats hard and tough as synonyms; in materials science they are distinct properties, and glass — hard yet brittle — shows why.

items.q: Why are ribs added to plastic mouldings?
items.correct: They increase stiffness and strength using very little extra material, keeping the moulding thin, light and quick to produce.
items.misconception: Ribs are added purely as decoration on the hidden side of products.
items.random_wrong: Ribs are added to make the plastic easier to melt down for recycling.
items.why: Students rarely connect geometry with stiffness; ribbing shows that shape, not just material choice, determines how a part resists bending.

items.q: What is the difference between tension and compression?
items.correct: Tension is a pulling force that stretches a material; compression is a pushing force that squashes it. A bent beam experiences both at once on opposite faces.
items.misconception: Tension and compression are the same force given different names in different materials.
items.random_wrong: Tension only occurs in metal components.
items.why: Students memorise force names without direction; linking pull-stretch and push-squash, and spotting both in bending, underpins all structural questions.
The answer examiners want is: Torsion. “Tension” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why are ribs moulded into plastic products?
items.correct: To increase stiffness without adding much weight
items.why: The answer examiners want is: To increase stiffness without adding much weight. “To make the product heavier” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why do designers use standard components such as screws and hinges?
items.correct: They are cheaper and more reliable than making custom equivalents
items.misconception: They make products harder to repair
items.random_wrong: They are required on every product by law
items.why: The answer examiners want is: They are cheaper and more reliable than making custom equivalents. “They make products harder to repair” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which scale of production runs 24 hours a day making one product, such as paper?
items.why: Correct answer: Continuous production. “One-off production” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
